title: March of Cambreadth

infobox.value: [*Radcon 5 draws sci-fi, fantasy fans to Pasco*](http://www.tri-cityherald.com/901/story/93016.html)[*[permanent dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*], Tri-City Herald, Feb 17, 2008. The article refers to "[t]ransgender Celtic performer Alexander James Adams", adding that "[t]he singer-songwriter toured across the U.S. and in England and Germany as Heather Alexander for 25 years before beginning to tour as Alexander, said Kore Adams, his partner.

[*Q & A with AJ Adams*](http://www.pac-fen.com/episodes/E116-Q_and_A_with_AJ_Adams/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100924083358/http://www.pac-fen.com/episodes/E116-Q_and_A_with_AJ_Adams/) 2010-09-24 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), Pacific Fen Spotlight, September 27, 2008. Alec discusses the fact that Heather Alexander was transgender.

[*Special Exclusive! March of Con Death*](http://www.pac-fen.com/episodes/E202x-March_of_Con_Death/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100924083236/http://www.pac-fen.com/episodes/E202x-March_of_Con_Death/) 2010-09-24 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), Pacific Fen Spotlight, Sep 21, 2009. The video starts with an introduction by the show's host and then moves on to Alexander giving a brief introduction of how the parody came about, crediting Theo Winters for the inspiration. Once the song is finished, an audience member identified by Alexander as Gene Armstrong asks if Alexander is going to record the song for posterity, to which the performer points his bow at the camera saying "We just did."

["Archived copy"](https://web.archive.org/web/20111121110624/http://sff.net/people/jcbunnell/lyrics.htm). Archived from [the original](http://www.sff.net/people/jcbunnell/lyrics.htm) on 2011-11-21. Retrieved 2011-07-11.`{{cite web}}`: CS1 maint: archived copy as title ([link](/wiki/Category:CS1_maint:_archived_copy_as_title "Category:CS1 maint: archived copy as title"))

first_paragraph: "**March of Cambreadth**" is the [signature song](/wiki/Signature_song "Signature song") of American singer, musician and songwriter [Alexander James Adams](/wiki/Alexander_James_Adams "Alexander James Adams"), previously known as Heather Alexander.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The song is well known in [filk](/wiki/Filk "Filk"), [Renaissance fair](/wiki/Renaissance_fair "Renaissance fair") and [Society for Creative Anachronism](/wiki/Society_for_Creative_Anachronism "Society for Creative Anachronism") circles. It has been featured in novels by Mike Shepherd, [John Ringo](/wiki/John_Ringo "John Ringo") and [S.M. Stirling](/wiki/S.M._Stirling "S.M. Stirling"). It has also been [parodied](/wiki/Parody "Parody") extensively.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"**March of Cambreadth**" is the [signature song](/wiki/Signature_song "Signature song") of American singer, musician and songwriter [Alexander James Adams](/wiki/Alexander_James_Adams "Alexander James Adams"), previously known as Heather Alexander.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The song is well known in [filk](/wiki/Filk "Filk"), [Renaissance fair](/wiki/Renaissance_fair "Renaissance fair") and [Society for Creative Anachronism](/wiki/Society_for_Creative_Anachronism "Society for Creative Anachronism") circles. It has been featured in novels by Mike Shepherd, [John Ringo](/wiki/John_Ringo "John Ringo") and [S.M. Stirling](/wiki/S.M._Stirling "S.M. Stirling"). It has also been [parodied](/wiki/Parody "Parody") extensively.

"March of Cambreadth" received a [Pegasus Award](/wiki/Pegasus_Award "Pegasus Award") in 2006, in the category "Best Battle Song".

## Recording history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=March_of_Cambreadth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Recording history")]

[Alexander James Adams](/wiki/Alexander_James_Adams "Alexander James Adams") wrote the song in the late 1980s and sang lead vocals on the following recordings:

* The 1990 Phoenyx album *Keepers of the Flame*. The band disbanded in 1991.
* The 1997 Heather Alexander solo album *Midsummer*, where it is framed as the center of the ["War Trilogy"](#War_Trilogy). The *Midsummer* recording is at a faster tempo than on *Keepers of the Flame* and the [Wicked Tinkers](/wiki/Wicked_Tinkers "Wicked Tinkers") add their bagpipe-and-drums sound to the song.
* [Uffington Horse](/wiki/Uffington_Horse_(band) "Uffington Horse (band)")'s *Enchantment* includes a live recording of "March of Cambreadth" with Andrew Hare playing banjo, [Dan Ochipinti](/w/index.php?title=Dan_Ochipinti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dan Ochipinti (page does not exist)") playing drums, and Heather Alexander switching between guitar and fiddle. (This recording is included in a computer-readable data track on the [Mixed Mode CD](/wiki/Mixed_Mode_CD "Mixed Mode CD").)

Alexander James Adams has sung lead vocals on the following recordings:

* Alexander James Adams and Tricky Pixie included the song on their 2007 album *Live!*. *Live!* is currently out of print.
* The 2010 Alexander James Adams studio album *Harvest Season: Second Cutting*.
* The Alexander James Adams live Yule DVD, recorded in December 2009.

The song also appears on albums by other artists.

## War Trilogy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=March_of_Cambreadth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: War Trilogy")]

The "War Trilogy" on *Midsummer* consists of three songs. The first, a love ballad, anticipates the battle; the second portrays the battle; the third looks back on the battle and its results.

* "Tomorrow I Leave For Battle," lyrics: Philip R. Obermarck, music: Heather Alexander
* "March of Cambreadth," lyrics & music: Heather Alexander
* "Courage Knows No Bounds," lyrics: Philip R. Obermarck, music: Heather Alexander

## "Hap'n'Frog of Cambreadth" and other parodies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=March_of_Cambreadth&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: \"Hap'n'Frog of Cambreadth\" and other parodies")]

As described on the live album *Festival Wind*, Alexander was reading an Internet filk mailing list when his fans observed that they could sing "March of Cambreadth" to the tune of his children's song "Hap'n'Frog" and vice versa. Determined to embarrass himself before anyone else did it for him, he took the two songs "and let them have an afternoon together and breed." The result is "Hap'n'Frog of Cambreadth," recorded on *Festival Wind*.

Alexander did a second self-parody "March of Con Death" specifically for the [RainFurrest](/wiki/RainFurrest "RainFurrest") 2009 Fur Suit Parade. As the theme for that year was "Zombie Attack" the lyrics included humorous puns relating to unlife and keeping the signature chorus line of "How many of them can we make die!"[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Alexander also performed the parody "December of Cambreadth" on the album *Roundworm*. The words to the parody are by Bob Kanefsky,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and deal with Santa Claus
delivering toys, replacing the signature refrain "How many of them can we make die?" with "How many of them can we bring toys?"

Two other filk songs based on March have been written, both dealing with the aftermath of battle with the refrain "How many of them can we make live?". One, written by Batya Wittenberg, is titled "Healer's Cry",[[5]](#cite_note-5) and the other, by John C. Bunnell, is titled "After Cambreadth".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=March_of_Cambreadth&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[John Ringo](/wiki/John_Ringo "John Ringo") has (mis)quoted "March of Cambreadth" in his novels *[Hell's Faire](/wiki/Hell%27s_Faire "Hell's Faire")*, *Ghost* and *There Will Be Dragons* as well as in the second *[Looking-Glass](/wiki/Voyage_of_the_Space_Bubble "Voyage of the Space Bubble")* book, *Vorpal Blade*. A copy of the *Midsummer* recording was included on CD-ROM in *There Will Be Dragons*, *Hell's Faire* and in the [Baen Free Library](/wiki/Baen_Free_Library "Baen Free Library").

[S.M. Stirling](/wiki/S.M._Stirling "S.M. Stirling") quoted or referenced "March of Cambreadth" in the [Emberverse](/wiki/Emberverse "Emberverse") volumes *[The Protector's War](/wiki/The_Protector%27s_War "The Protector's War")*, *[A Meeting at Corvallis](/wiki/A_Meeting_at_Corvallis "A Meeting at Corvallis")*, *[The Sunrise Lands](/wiki/The_Sunrise_Lands "The Sunrise Lands")*, and the Nantucket volume *[On the Oceans of Eternity](/wiki/On_the_Oceans_of_Eternity "On the Oceans of Eternity")*, in all of which he credits Alexander while having his characters refer to the song as traditional. The composer, who was then known as Heather Alexander, licensed their image for the basis of the character Juniper Mackenzie, in addition to song lyrics.

[Mike Shepherd](/wiki/Mike_Shepherd_(author) "Mike Shepherd (author)") used the song in his book *[Kris Longknife: Defiant](/w/index.php?title=Kris_Longknife:_Defiant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kris Longknife: Defiant (page does not exist)")*.

[Bob Kanefsky](/w/index.php?title=Bob_Kanefsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bob Kanefsky (page does not exist)") has parodied *March of Cambreadth* twice:

* "Weight Loss Centers from Hell"
* "December of Cambreadth"

Heather Alexander recorded "December of Cambreadth" for the compilation album *Roundworm*.

P. R. Frost quoted "March of Cambreadth" in the book *Moon In The Mirror: A Tess Noncoiré Adventure*.

Because of his Celtic tune and his use of bagpipes, March of Cambreadth was taken by the supporters of the [Gaunt's Ghosts](/wiki/Gaunt%27s_Ghosts "Gaunt's Ghosts") saga and used in [fanon](/wiki/Canon_(fiction)#Fanon "Canon (fiction)") for the battle hymn of the "Tanith First-and-Only", sung by the trooper Brin Milo to encourage and motivate his companions in the hellish battlefields of the 41st Millennium ([Warhammer 40,000](/wiki/Warhammer_40,000 "Warhammer 40,000")).
